title: **Balance Sheet Overview – As of May 31, 2026**
content:
The primary driver of the credit union’s balance sheet movement was a sharp contraction in loans and leases, which declined by $2.2 million or 11.1% year over year to stand at $19.8 million. This reduction pulled total assets down slightly despite offsetting gains elsewhere; overall assets grew just 7.8% to reach $63.7 million.

Investment securities expanded significantly, rising by $4.5 million or 13.2% to become the largest single asset category at $39.3 million, thereby expanding the institution’s earning-asset base outside of direct lending. Cash and deposits surged dramatically — up $608 thousand or 247.6% to $3.6 million — which improved near-term liquidity as funds moved into this low-risk holding. Other assets contracted by $79 thousand (–9.1%), while other investments remained flat.

Loans and leases now represent the largest remaining asset category after securities, though their decline means they are no longer dominant in dollar terms; however, investment securities have overtaken them as the top balance sheet item. The funding side matched this shift: total liabilities shrank 5.5% to $48.0 million, primarily driven by a drop of $3.1 million (–6.3%) in shares/deposits.

Total equity stood at $15.7 million, with undivided earnings equaling that exact amount and reserves showing no separate line item or change noted; this reflects the credit union’s capital position as measured by retained earnings alone. The combined effect of asset growth concentrated in securities and cash, paired with a pullback in both loans and member deposits, leaves the institution better capitalized but less reliant on traditional lending for returns.

The board should monitor whether loan demand stabilizes to reverse the contraction seen this period.
